messages.author: paul / BostonMesh
messages.text: @RocketMan quick thought on pulling repeater stats - consider storing the values locally so that you can do some data analysis. I’m logging in to see if battery has gone up/down, check on the neighbors etc. It would be interesting if you added some spark lines to see trends over time. Are the repeaters stronger/weaker/etc. How much has the battery gone up/down, etc. Maybe spark lines for the packets Tx/Rx’d etc. Every other client view the status page as simple point in time display of the data - if the client is pulling it down, automatically storing it would be handy. You could display Tufte-esque spark lines next to the values, or simply have a historical “status” page for all of the data points.
messages.content_html: @RocketMan quick thought on pulling repeater stats - consider storing the values locally so that you can do some data analysis. I’m logging in to see if battery has gone up/down, check on the neighbors etc. It would be interesting if you added some spark lines to see trends over time. Are the repeaters stronger/weaker/etc. How much has the battery gone up/down, etc. Maybe spark lines for the packets Tx/Rx’d etc. Every other client view the status page as simple point in time display of the data - if the client is pulling it down, automatically storing it would be handy. You could display Tufte-esque spark lines next to the values, or simply have a historical “status” page for all of the data points.

messages.author: Just Stuff™
messages.forwarded_html:
Forwarded
I use this to discover repeaters in the Web app. mesh-utility.replit.app function buildNodeDiscoverReq(filter: number): Uint8Array { const tag = Math.floor(Math.random() * 0xFFFFFFFF); const data = new Uint8Array(1 + 1 + 1 + 4); data[0] = CMD_SEND_CONTROL_DATA; data[1] = CONTROL_NODE_DISCOVER_REQ | 0x01; data[2] = filter; data[3] = tag & 0xFF; data[4] = (tag >> 8) & 0xFF; data[5] = (tag >> 16) & 0xFF; data[6] = (tag >> 24) & 0xFF; remoteLog("log", `node_discover cmd: ${Array.from(data).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}, filter=${filter}, tag=${tag}`); return data; } function parseControlDataFrame(frame: Uint8Array): NodeDiscoverEntry | null { if (frame.length < 5 || frame[0] !== PUSH_CODE_CONTROL_DATA) return null; const view = new DataView(frame.buffer, frame.byteOffset, frame.byteLength); const snrRaw = view.getInt8(1); const snr = snrRaw / 4; const rssi = view.getInt8(2); const pathLen = frame[3]; const payload = frame.slice(4); remoteLog("log", `ControlData raw: snr=${snr} rssi=${rssi} pathLen=${pathLen} payload(${payload.length}b)=${Array.from(payload).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}`); if (payload.length < 1) { remoteLog("log", `ControlData: empty payload`); return null; }

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: I am asking this for all companion applications. could you make sending messages in multi-byte option per channel and per contact? I imagine for a while even after all repeaters are updated to 1.14+, there will be channels you want to send with 1-byte to get 64 hops, and then there will be channels you want to send with 2- or 3-byte to disembogue repeater IDs and fewer hops. Another example: as a regional mesh updating their repeaters to 1.14+, I may want to create a #multibytepublic hashtag channel to test multibyte with others in public. We can do this to test 1.14 repeater firmware take rate and coverage. We would need to send only multibyte messages in #multibyte_public, but keep regular public channel in 1-byte path hash.
